body_txt: A person who breaks the law, for their own personal profit, but is nice enough and charming enough to allow the audience to root for them, especially if they don't kill or otherwise seriously harm anyone. It's often made clear that their targets are Asshole Victims the audience won't sympathize with, especially if the Lovable Rogue is a killer of some sort. For extra points, they might even give some of their takings to the poor . The most legitimate way to make this trope work is by making the rogue a Justified Criminal who turns to crime only to survive in an uncaring world that leaves them with no other option, especially if the laws are unfair and benefit a select few at the expense of others, including the rogue. Of course, the most pragmatic reason for this trope is that it allows a criminal character to be a protagonist—be it a teammate, love interest , or even The Hero —by not having them do anything actually bad . It might be implied that they committed worse crimes in the past, but nothing we see onscreen in the present day would lose them sympathy points with the audience. Thus, the Lovable Rogue can help save the day without becoming a Karma Houdini (too much). Note that while morally a click below your average antihero in the sense that they might not be considered a hero at all, the Lovable Rogue is strongly associated with highly Idealistic series on the Sliding Scale of Idealism Versus Cynicism , as Cynical series tend not to place value on the concept of a Code of Honor , which is usually what makes the Lovable Rogue, well, lovable. Their loyalties are often played with, particularly if they show up to rescue the hero . If they end up being the star of the show, expect a straight-arrow Supporting Protagonist to be the audience viewpoint character. Complicating matters further is the fact that this person is usually chased by a Sympathetic Inspector Antagonist . This character is a click above the Lovable Traitor , who is definitely not a nice person, but wins by lowering the audience's expectations so much, that he charms them with a few token Pet the Dog moments (which usually include helping the hero. It's amazing how much fans will overlook if they help the hero). The g comes before the u , folks. That is, unless you're talking about Rouge the Bat , who just happens to be a good example of this trope, in more ways than one. If the charming person is a villain instead of an Anti-Hero , then they are described as being Affably Evil . The audience will usually not root for a villain, even if he or she is Affably Evil . Usually. See also Gentleman Thief , Karmic Thief , Venturous Smuggler , Roguish Poacher , Indispensable Scoundrel , Justified Criminal , and Just Like Robin Hood . Contrast Evil Hero , where a person with a Noble Profession is evil.
